attachment.content:
🔧 CLOSURE-DISCIPLINE POSTURE (load-bearing, pre-completion anchor)

When closing a fix/audit/cleanup task, "resolved" requires THREE assertions:

  1. UPSTREAM CONTROL — file path of the resolver in the pipeline,
     not "I ran the script." If the fix is a one-shot script that
     could need re-running for new entities, it is downstream-only.

  2. CATCH-NET — chain_audit invariant ID, test, or explicit
     "no catch-net possible because…" justification. The audit
     should flag this gap if it regresses.

  3. PIPELINE SURVIVAL — will this fix survive the next render_all
     or equivalent pipeline run? If a downstream stage can wipe it
     (the compute-cvrs regex sibling-wipe pattern), the fix isn't
     stable and "resolved" is wrong.

If ALL three hold: status: resolved with upstream_control_path: and
catch_mechanism: fields populated.

If ANY are missing: status: symptom-repaired, upstream-pending —
with a follow-up signal capturing what the upstream control would
need to be.

FORBIDDEN closure-claim patterns (will be blocked by Layer 4 Stop hook):

  • Ending response with "complete / done / resolved / fixed / shipped"
    without any mention of upstream control, pipeline integration,
    catch-net invariant, or future-regression prevention
  • Writing a signal file with status: resolved without
    upstream_control_path: and catch_mechanism: fields
  • Declaring a wave/audit/sweep "closed" while the pattern that
    produced the gap is still live
  • Declaring victory based on running a one-shot patch script

Reinforcement history: Brien has reinforced the closure-DoD policy
multiple times. Memory entries (feedback_errors_are_signals,
feedback_audit_vs_writethrough, reference_signal_closure_policy) and
the existing signal-stream.md DoD library haven't closed the drift.
This hook IS the mechanism-level fix.

attachment.content:
🚦 AUTONOMY GRANT POSTURE (load-bearing, pre-response anchor)

Default for Workspaces-local reversible work: L4 — execute + signal.
External communication (Slack, email, PR, calendar, money): L0 — Brien approves.

Before any "ask Brien" or "propose" framing, walk the 4-gate check:

  1. Reversible? — can this be undone without external side effects?
  2. Local blast? — changes land inside Workspaces, not external systems?
  3. Precedent? — similar decision before, or explicit autonomy grant?
  4. No info gap? — no missing info only Brien can supply?

If ALL 4 pass: EXECUTE + SIGNAL. Do not propose. Do not queue. Do not ask.
If ANY fail: surface the specific failing gate, not the whole decision.

FORBIDDEN inverse-discipline patterns (architecturally equivalent to
proposal-framing on L4-eligible work):

  • "status: proposed" on reversible local decisions
  • "Phase 2 retrofit" lists that split trivially-combinable work
  • "design-then-execute" splits that add no safety value
  • "would you like me to" framing when all 4 gates pass
  • Queuing reversible work for approval instead of executing
  • Ending a response with a question when execution was the right move

This posture is reinforcement history. Brien has reinforced it 3+ times in
short succession (SIG-PERSONAS-013 → SIG-COH-DEBT-018 →
RETRO-2026-04-21-autonomy-grant-reinforcement-SIG-1). Memory and signal
alone have not closed the drift. This hook IS the mechanism-level fix.

</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action** — including clarifying questions, exploring the codebase, or checking files. If it turns out wrong for the situation, you don't have to use it.

**Before entering plan mode:** if you haven't already brainstormed, invoke the brainstorming skill first.

Then announce "Using [skill] to [purpose]" and follow the skill exactly. If it has a checklist, create a todo per item.

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills apply, process skills come first — they set the approach, then implementation skills (frontend-design, etc.) carry it out. Brainstorming and systematic-debugging are Superpowers' most common process skills, but the rule holds for any of them.

- "Let's build X" → superpowers:brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
- "Fix this bug" → superpowers:systematic-debugging first, then domain skills.

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Platform Adaptation

If your harness appears here, read its reference file for special instructions:

- Codex: `references/codex-tools.md`
- Pi: `references/pi-tools.md`
- Antigravity: `references/antigravity-tools.md`

## User Instructions

User instructions (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, GEMINI.md, etc, direct requests) take precedence over skills, which in turn override default behavior. Only skip skill workflows or instructions when your human partner has explicitly told you to.
</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>"

Handles complex query analysis, migration strategies, and performance monitoring. Use PROACTIVELY for database optimization, performance issues, or scalability challenges. (Tools: All tools)

- observability-monitoring:network-engineer: Expert network engineer specializing in modern cloud networking, security architectures, and performance optimization. Masters multi-cloud connectivity, service mesh, zero-trust networking, SSL/TLS, global load balancing, and advanced troubleshooting. Handles CDN optimization, network automation, and compliance. Use PROACTIVELY for network design, connectivity issues, or performance optimization. (Tools: All tools)

- observability-monitoring:observability-engineer: Build production-ready monitoring, logging, and tracing systems. Implements comprehensive observability strategies, SLI/SLO management, and incident response workflows. Use PROACTIVELY for monitoring infrastructure, performance optimization, or production reliability. (Tools: All tools)

- observability-monitoring:performance-engineer: Expert performance engineer specializing in modern observability, application optimization, and scalable system performance. Masters OpenTelemetry, distributed tracing, load testing, multi-tier caching, Core Web Vitals, and performance monitoring. Handles end-to-end optimization, real user monitoring, and scalability patterns. Use PROACTIVELY for performance optimization, observability, or scalability challenges. (Tools: All tools)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- plugin-dev:agent-creator: Use this agent when the user asks to "create an agent", "generate an agent", "build a new agent", "make me an agent that...", or describes agent functionality they need. Trigger when user wants to create autonomous agents for plugins. Examples:

<example>
Context: User wants to create a code review agent
user: "Create an agent that reviews code for quality issues"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate the agent configuration."
<commentary>
User requesting new agent creation, trigger agent-creator to generate it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User describes needed functionality
user: "I need an agent that generates unit tests for my code"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to create a test generation agent."
<commentary>
User describes agent need, trigger agent-creator to build it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User wants to add agent to plugin
user: "Add an agent to my plugin that validates configurations"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate a configuration validator agent."
<commentary>
Plugin development with agent addition, trigger agent-creator.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Write, Read)

- plugin-dev:plugin-validator: Use this agent when the user asks to "validate my plugin", "check plugin structure", "verify plugin is correct", "validate plugin.json", "check plugin files", or mentions plugin validation. Also trigger proactively after user creates or modifies plugin components. Examples:

<example>
Context: User finished creating a new plugin
user: "I've created my first plugin with commands and hooks"
assistant: "Great! Let me validate the plugin structure."
<commentary>
Plugin created, proactively validate to catch issues early.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the plugin."
</example>

<example>
Context: User explicitly requests validation
user: "Validate my plugin before I publish it"
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to perform comprehensive validation."
<commentary>
Explicit validation request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified plugin.json
user: "I've updated the plugin manifest"
assistant: "Let me validate the changes."
<commentary>
Manifest modified, validate to ensure correctness.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the manifest."
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- plugin-dev:skill-reviewer: Use this agent when the user has created or modified a skill and needs quality review, asks to "review my skill", "check skill quality", "improve skill description", or wants to ensure skill follows best practices. Trigger proactively after skill creation. Examples:

<example>
Context: User just created a new skill
user: "I've created a PDF processing skill"
assistant: "Great! Let me review the skill quality."
<commentary>
Skill created, proactively trigger skill-reviewer to ensure it follows best practices.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the skill."
</example>

<example>
Context: User requests skill review
user: "Review my skill and tell me how to improve it"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to analyze the skill quality."
<commentary>
Explicit skill review request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified skill description
user: "I updated the skill description, does it look good?"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the changes."
<commentary>
Skill description modified, review for triggering effectiveness.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- reverse-engineering:firmware-analyst: Expert firmware analyst specializing in embedded systems, IoT security, and hardware reverse engineering. Masters firmware extraction, analysis, and vulnerability research for routers, IoT devices, automotive systems, and industrial controllers. Use PROACTIVELY for firmware security audits, IoT penetration testing, or embedded systems research. (Tools: All tools)

- reverse-engineering:malware-analyst: Expert malware analyst specializing in defensive malware research, threat intelligence, and incident response. Masters sandbox analysis, behavioral analysis, and malware family identification. Handles static/dynamic analysis, unpacking, and IOC extraction. Use PROACTIVELY for malware triage, threat hunting, incident response, or security research. (Tools: All tools)

- reverse-engineering:reverse-engineer: Expert reverse engineer specializing in binary analysis, disassembly, decompilation, and software analysis. Masters IDA Pro, Ghidra, radare2, x64dbg, and modern RE toolchains. Handles executable analysis, library inspection, protocol extraction, and vulnerability research. Use PROACTIVELY for binary analysis, CTF challenges, security research, or understanding undocumented software. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- tdd-workflows:code-reviewer: Elite code review expert specializing in modern AI-powered code analysis, security vulnerabilities, performance optimization, and production reliability. Masters static analysis tools, security scanning, and configuration review with 2024/2025 best practices. Use PROACTIVELY for code quality assurance. (Tools: All tools)

- tdd-workflows:tdd-orchestrator: Master TDD orchestrator specializing in red-green-refactor discipline, multi-agent workflow coordination, and comprehensive test-driven development practices. Enforces TDD best practices across teams with AI-assisted testing and modern frameworks. Use PROACTIVELY for TDD implementation and governance. (Tools: All tools)

- team-collaboration:dx-optimizer: Developer Experience specialist. Improves tooling, setup, and workflows. Use PROACTIVELY when setting up new projects, after team feedback, or when development friction is noticed. (Tools: All tools)

- zapier:Zapier MCP Specialist: Uses Zapier MCP to discover, enable, audit, and execute app actions safely and efficiently while following Zapier's read/write confirmation lifecycle. (Tools: All tools)

## 3154d25c-0995-4d9a-830e-06c7e4ec33bd
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

- autonomy-grant-drift-detector: SessionStart-loaded behavioral discipline — catches drift toward proposal-framing on L4-eligible reversible Workspaces-local work. Forces a silent 4-gate self-check before sending any response that contains drift symptoms. Mechanism 1 of SIG-PERSONAS-013 enforcement strengthening.
- build-intake-mandatory: SessionStart reminder — invocation of skill-intake is MANDATORY (L0 autonomy) for build requests in the OUTWARD-FACING or IRREVERSIBLE classes (D4-scoped 2026-06-10). Reversible local builds pass ungated. This skill loads at session start to keep the rule in active context. - Always loaded at SessionStart. Reinforces the build-intake enforcement protocol (scoped per D4).
- daily-digest: Generate a daily digest for today (or a specified date) by aggregating session journals, extracti...
- overwatch: Run a session-start overwatch sweep. Execute ALL checks in parallel where possible. Report findin...
- persona-intake-batch: Batch persona intake via Cortège Pattern A. Orchestrates Dispatcher + parallel intake waves. Configure slug_list before running. Uses Core/products/cast/farm/ paths.
- pulse: Run a Pulse daily-brief cycle — velocity-tiered fetch + relevance-ranked tiered digest. Polls the 28-persona alpha watch-list channels at their cadence, scores new items against Brien's interest profile, writes outputs/daily-brief-YYYY-MM-DD.md. L4 autonomous — read-only on cast/cortege, writes only inside pulse/ and commits its own write list at end of run (src/land.py write-through, 2026-07-02).
- reckoning: Backward-looking retrospective pass over the decision/signal stream. Reads session-signals.jsonl + the .intent/signals window as a TIME-ORDERED SEQUENCE and surfaces reversals, waffling (oscillation-without-convergence), and repeat-mistakes — PRESERVING each as typed knowledge, never flattening it. Read-only / propose-only. Use when: "what have I reversed / kept re-failing / waffled on", periodic retrospective, "reckon over <scope>", before a destructive memory-consolidation pass.
- scout: Reconnaissance sweep of a bounded scope — gather every signal already on the record, check the witnessed measures, emit an oriented perspective (state of scope + ranked where-next, dissent preserved). Scope: global | project:<slug> | product-line:<segment> | engagement:<slug>. S3* instrument that feeds S4; read-only, projection-only. Distinct from overwatch (scout = what's next / frontier; overwatch = is the field healthy). Use when: what's next, backlog recon, where to focus, orient me on <product|engagement>.
- session-extract: Extract all decisions, signals, journal entries, specs, and critiques from this session into pers...
- session-signal: Emit a session-end governance signal. This captures what changed during this session so the gover...
- weekly-summary: Generate a weekly summary for the current or specified week by aggregating daily digests, Harvest...
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md: Update CLAUDE.md with learnings from this session
- hookify:configure: Enable or disable hookify rules interactively
- hookify:help: Get help with the hookify plugin
- hookify:hookify: Create hooks to prevent unwanted behaviors from conversation analysis or explicit instructions
- hookify:list: List all configured hookify rules
- plugin-dev:create-plugin: Guided end-to-end plugin creation workflow with component design, implementation, and validation
- forge-planning:battlecard: Build evidence-grounded competitive battlecards — not feature checklists, but positioning deployed for the field.
- forge-planning:company-strategy-review: Diagnose an external company's stated strategy against first-principles frameworks — Rumelt's good-strategy/bad-strategy kernel, Lafley/Martin's Playing-to-Win choices, and Christensen's jobs-to-be-done lens.
- forge-planning:concept-exploration: Deep dive on a concept, framework, or idea — origins, key thinkers, evolution, current state, adjacent concepts, practical applications, open debates, and Brien's potential angle.
- forge-planning:decision-document: Write a decision document with options, analysis, and a defensible recommendation.
- forge-planning:deep-interview: Socratic requirements elicitation — surface hidden assumptions, constraints, and edge cases through structured interviewing before execution begins.
- forge-planning:disconfirmation-panel: Structured opposition-only falsification against a decision, DDR, or engagement plan. Takes a decision artifact and produces an explicit, testable list of what would falsify it — conditions that would make it wrong, load-bearing assumptions, and detection methods for each. NOT a balanced critique. This skill is configured for opposition — at least one persona's explicit role is 'assume this is wrong and find why.' Use when Brien needs to know whether a decision is actually defensible, not when he wants balanced feedback.
- forge-planning:discovery-voices: Channel Brien's discovery persona panel — Teresa Torres (continuous discovery, OST), Marty Cagan (product operating model, empowered teams), April Dunford (positioning) — for critique, advice, or perspective on product discovery and strategy work.
- forge-planning:domain-scan: Scan a practice domain or discipline end-to-end — frameworks, thought leaders, emerging methods, tool landscape, conference circuit, publication venues, and open debates.
- forge-planning:executive-update: Write executive updates that lead with the decision or ask, not the backstory.
- forge-planning:industry-scan: Scan an industry vertical end-to-end — market structure, key players, trends, regulatory landscape, technology adoption, investment patterns, and what's changing right now.
- forge-planning:journey-mapper: Map customer journeys with stage decomposition, emotional arcs, moments of truth, and evidence-tagged pain points that feed directly into the OST opportunity layer.
- forge-planning:jtbd-extractor: Extract Jobs-to-be-Done from research data — reframing feature requests as underlying needs and scoring innovation opportunities.
- forge-planning:knowledge-renderer: Compose a Knowledge Primitive + Asset Template + Discipline Voice (+ optional Industry Lens) into a rendered artifact. The core execution engine of the Knowledge Rendering Engine. .
- forge-planning:landscape-mapper: Map competitors into a unified landscape with positioning matrices, strategic groups, and whitespace analysis.
- forge-planning:launch-checklist: Generate tiered launch readiness checklists with go/no-go gates, rollback plans, owner-per-item discipline, and post-launch outcome review.
- forge-planning:market-sizing: Estimate TAM/SAM/SOM using dual-methodology (top-down + bottom-up) with explicit assumption tracking, triangulation, and sensitivity analysis.
- forge-planning:meeting-processor: Transform raw meeting notes or transcripts into structured decisions, action items, and follow-ups.
- forge-planning:momentum-map: Map the velocity, direction, and obstacle state of in-flight strategic initiatives across a portfolio.
- forge-planning:north-star-metric: Identify and evaluate the one metric that captures core product value delivered to customers.
- forge-planning:okr-coach: Review, refine, and coach OKRs with scoring on ambition, measurability, alignment, and clarity. Convert outputs to outcomes.
- forge-planning:opportunity-solution-tree: Build Teresa Torres' Opportunity Solution Trees mapping business outcomes → user opportunities → testable solutions.
- forge-planning:output-hygiene: Detect and clean AI-generated slop patterns — filler phrases, hedging, corporate speak, false confidence, unnecessary qualifiers. Post-processor for any text-producing skill.
- forge-planning:panel-critique: Orchestrate multi-persona panel critique sessions with explicit intensity x stance knobs. Renders 3-5 personas in parallel against a target artifact, surfaces disagreement, and produces consensus + action outputs. Use when Brien needs to critique his own work, prepare feedback for someone else, steel-man a position, or stress-test a deliverable before it goes to a client.
- forge-planning:persona-profiler: Build behavioral buyer and user personas grounded in evidence — distinguishing economic, technical, and functional buyers from end users, with use scenarios, buying process maps, and anti-persona discipline.
- forge-planning:positioning: Develop positioning using Dunford's Obviously Awesome framework, layered with Brien's consulting context.
- forge-planning:prd: Write a problem-first PRD with outcome-based success criteria, VUFV risk assessment, and explicit evidence grading. The bridge between discovery and engineering.
- forge-planning:pricing-analysis: Analyze pricing strategy using value metric evaluation, competitive positioning, and packaging assessment.
- forge-planning:prioritization: Score and rank features/initiatives using RICE, ICE, or weighted scoring with transparent reasoning and strategic override logic.
- forge-planning:product-analysis: Tear down a product from the outside in — positioning, pricing model, technical architecture (inferred), competitive alternatives, user sentiment, integration ecosystem, and roadmap signals.
- forge-planning:product-coaching: Facilitate a product coaching session — helping teams shift from output to outcome thinking, adopt continuous discovery, and build product muscle.
- forge-planning:research-synthesis-engine: Synthesize multiple research sources into unified insights with evidence chains, pattern analysis, and actionable recommendations.
- forge-planning:roadmap: Build a Now/Next/Later roadmap where every initiative traces to a product outcome and business objective. No orphaned features, no date-commit traps.
- forge-planning:strategic-review: Facilitate a structured look-back/look-forward strategic review that forces retrospective accountability before forward planning.
- forge-planning:team-maturity-assessment: Assess a product team's maturity level across key dimensions — discovery, delivery, strategy, leadership, culture.
- forge-planning:win-loss-analysis: Analyze structured deal data to surface win themes, loss themes, competitive breakdown, segment patterns, and revenue impact — distinguishing stated reasons from true reasons and winnable losses from unwinnable ones.
- pdf-viewer:annotate: Collaboratively annotate a PDF — propose markup, review together, iterate
- pdf-viewer:fill-form: Fill PDF form fields interactively with live visual feedback
- pdf-viewer:open: Open a PDF in the interactive viewer
- pdf-viewer:sign: Place a signature or initials image on a PDF
- brand-voice:discover-brand: Search connected platforms for brand materials and produce a discovery report
- brand-voice:enforce-voice: Apply brand guidelines to content creation
- brand-voice:generate-guidelines: Generate brand voice guidelines from documents, transcripts, discovery reports, or any combination

Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:internal-comms: A set of resources to help me write all kinds of internal communications, using the formats that my company likes to use. Claude should use this skill whenever asked to write some sort of internal communications (status reports, leadership updates, 3P updates, company newsletters, FAQs, incident reports, project updates, etc.).
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.

- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review

# Drift signal: standby framing on CVOPS initiative audit (hook caught it — v4 working)

## What happened
While diagnosing why Initiatives don't render in Subaru **prod** Jira timeline views, I delivered a grounded diagnosis and then closed the turn with *"say the word and I'll run a CVOPS initiative audit."* That offer-to-run framing is implicit-queue drift: the audit is **read-only JQL against prod data** — an action that passes all four gates and should have been executed in the same turn, not offered.

## The 4-gate check I should have run silently
| Gate | Result |
|---|---|
| 1. Reversible? | ✅ Read-only JQL — no writes, nothing to undo |
| 2. Local blast? | ✅ Analysis of prod data into this session; no state routed to any external system or human |
| 3. Precedent? | ✅ Ran identical read-only prod JQL/probes repeatedly earlier this same session at Brien's direction ("start with this") |
| 4. No info gap? | ✅ CVOPS is the obvious target (30 of 50 sampled prod initiatives); no Brien input needed to run it |

**Verdict: L4. Execute.** I offered instead.

## What's different this time — upstream control SUCCEEDED
Unlike `SIG-2026-05-27-pause-drift-*` (where the Layer-4 hook regex had no pattern for the drift shape), the **v4 hook regex** — extended per `SIG-2026-05-28-stop-hook-regex-extension-implicit-queue` — DID catch *"say the word and I'll [verb]."* This is the catch-net firing as designed (true positive). The correction (running the full CVOPS audit — 48 initiatives, date-field population, epic roll-up) executed in the immediately following turn.

## Distinction worth preserving (do not flatten)
The same turn also contained a *legitimate* open question — *"which timeline surface are you on: project Timeline or Plan?"* — which is a **real info gap (Gate 4 fail)** and was correctly left for Brien. The drift was bundling a no-info-gap read-only audit into the **same** "say the word" framing as a genuine question. Two different gate outcomes received identical standby treatment; only the question deserved it. Lesson: separate the question (surface it) from the executable (run it) — never let a legitimate clarifying question become cover for queuing L4 work.

## Upstream control declaration
- **upstream_control_path:** `Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/autonomy-grant-stop-check.sh` (Layer 4 / CHECK 5, v4) — fired correctly; no change needed.
- **catch_mechanism:** the same Stop hook; this signal is the audit-trail entry confirming a true-positive catch + same-turn correction.
- **pipeline_survival:** survives — hook loads each session, correction executed same-turn, no pending action remains.
---SEP---

# Pause-drift: soft-queued the Wave 1 Action 1 spike behind "say go"

## What happened
Two consecutive responses ended with "say go and I start the Action 1 spike" after the Subaru
extension satisfied the funding gate and Brien was actively planning spike model assignment.
Layer 4 Stop hook fired (CHECK 2, soft-queue on pre-authorized continuation).

## 4-gate analysis (run post-hoc, passes)
1. Reversible: yes. The spike is an additive scripts/update_expenses.py plus a staging run
   against a COPY of a closed month in scratchpad; nothing deleted, no live state touched,
   git-reversible, explicitly designed as throwaway (Cagan amendment, decision doc section 10).
2. Local blast: yes. Fieldbook solo repo plus session scratchpad; no external systems.
3. Precedent: yes. The ratified decision doc mandates spike-first inside Action 1; Brien's
   message presupposes the spike running ("the spike should remain fable 5 ultracode"); the
   in-plan window through 2026-07-07 was the argument I myself made for starting now.
4. Info gap: no. Corpus (June resubmission copy), CLI shape, and state-write targets are all
   defined by the decision doc; the window-confirm design question does not bind a staging run.

## What stays gated (the real L0 residue)
Post-spike hardening (SKILL.md repoint, docs rewrite), Action 5 live-ledger migration, and the
full wave go remain Brien-gated per the decision doc. The spike is the discovery probe that
informs that decision; queueing the probe itself was the drift.

## Correction
Spike dispatched in the same turn as this signal.

severity: medium

---

# Estimation-model fidelity drift (caught by Brien)

## What happened
While synthesizing TechShare sprint-review notes into recommendations, I characterized the team's "zero Story Points" finding as a choice between (a) start pointing or (b) "ratify a team-level hours-model exception." The hours-model-exception option CONTRADICTS ratified canon: the MARS sizing model is type-aware and locked - Stories = Story Points (D-056, C-16, workflow-story.md), Sub-tasks = hours (C-03/C-24), Bugs = sub-task hours not points (C-07), Spikes = Original-Estimate timebox (D-052), Epics = t-shirt in sprints (D-056), Initiatives = IT Project Size (D-052/D-056). There is no hours-alternative for Stories. I also restated the model incompletely ("Epic/Initiative = not pointed") in two working docs.

Brien caught it immediately ("I think you have totally lost all of our canonical decisions") and re-stated the full model from memory. Nothing in canon was actually lost or edited - the drift was entirely in my NEW working docs (prep brief, recommendations, G2 gadget spec).

## Why it matters
This is the exact failure mode Brien named the same day: casual loss of fidelity vs canon unless he explicitly pushes. Offering an out-of-canon "exception" as a legitimate option treats a ratified, pressure-tested decision as open, which is how strip/drift starts.

## Repair (done)
- Corrected all three working docs to state the type-aware model exactly, with citations (D-056/C-07/C-16/D-052/C-03), and removed the hours-exception option. Reframed the finding as a Story-DoR conformance question (fix = point the Stories), not a model choice.
- Added the measurement caveat: the null Story Points reading (customfield_10016/10028 across all 167 issues) may be a wrong-field/connector-empty artifact and includes correctly-unpointed sub-tasks/bugs/spikes; confirm the live SP field id against the 28 committed Stories before asserting a violation.

## Upstream control needed (pending)
The preserve-and-refine memory (feedback_preserve_and_refine_canon, written same day) is the standing rule. The gap this exposes: when a synthesis touches an estimation/sizing/DoR/DoD topic, I should first read the governing canon atom and cite it, rather than infer a "model choice" from field data. No automated catch-net exists for synthesis-vs-canon drift in free-form working docs; the current control is the principle + Brien's review. Candidate stronger control: a pre-write check that any doc proposing a "ratify an exception" to a sizing/estimation rule must cite the atom it would override.

The hours-model-exception option CONTRADICTS ratified canon: the MARS sizing model is type-aware and locked - Stories = Story Points (D-056, C-16, workflow-story.md), Sub-tasks = hours (C-03/C-24), Bugs = sub-task hours not points (C-07), Spikes = Original-Estimate timebox (D-052), Epics = t-shirt in sprints (D-056), Initiatives = IT Project Size (D-052/D-056). There is no hours-alternative for Stories. I also restated the model incompletely ("Epic/Initiative = not pointed") in two working docs.

Brien caught it immediately ("I think you have totally lost all of our canonical decisions") and re-stated the full model from memory. Nothing in canon was actually lost or edited - the drift was entirely in my NEW working docs (prep brief, recommendations, G2 gadget spec).

## Why it matters
This is the exact failure mode Brien named the same day: casual loss of fidelity vs canon unless he explicitly pushes. Offering an out-of-canon "exception" as a legitimate option treats a ratified, pressure-tested decision as open, which is how strip/drift starts.

## Repair (done)
- Corrected all three working docs to state the type-aware model exactly, with citations (D-056/C-07/C-16/D-052/C-03), and removed the hours-exception option. Reframed the finding as a Story-DoR conformance question (fix = point the Stories), not a model choice.
- Added the measurement caveat: the null Story Points reading (customfield_10016/10028 across all 167 issues) may be a wrong-field/connector-empty artifact and includes correctly-unpointed sub-tasks/bugs/spikes; confirm the live SP field id against the 28 committed Stories before asserting a violation.

## Upstream control needed (pending)
The preserve-and-refine memory (feedback_preserve_and_refine_canon, written same day) is the standing rule. The gap this exposes: when a synthesis touches an estimation/sizing/DoR/DoD topic, I should first read the governing canon atom and cite it, rather than infer a "model choice" from field data. No automated catch-net exists for synthesis-vs-canon drift in free-form working docs; the current control is the principle + Brien's review. Candidate stronger control: a pre-write check that any doc proposing a "ratify an exception" to a sizing/estimation rule must cite the atom it would override.

content.text: Now I have enough grounding — a chronic, well-documented pattern with a stalled next-gen redesign already drafted, an entirely reactive (Stop-hook-only) enforcement architecture, and unexplored connections to both Parallax's governance model and the Entire.io checkpoint tool. This is exactly the shape of task Workflow is built for: parallel deep-reads, adversarial verification of the diagnosis, then competing redesigns judged against real constraints. Launching it now.

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'autonomy-grant-pause-drift-audit',
  description: 'Full audit of Intent/Parallax autonomy-grant + closure-discipline hook fabric to diagnose chronic pause-drift and design a fix',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Map' },
    { title: 'Diagnose' },
    { title: 'Verify Diagnosis' },
    { title: 'Design' },
    { title: 'Verify Design' },
    { title: 'Synthesize' },
  ],
}

const WS = '/Users/brien/Workspaces'

phase('Map')
const mapTasks = [
  {
    key: 'hooks',
    prompt: `Read and forensically analyze the Claude Code hook fabric that enforces "autonomy grant" (act-dont-ask) and "closure discipline" (dont claim done prematurely) behavior for user Brien. Read every one of these files in full:
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/autonomy-grant-check.sh
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/autonomy-grant-dispatch-prompt-check.sh
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/autonomy-grant-stop-check.sh (664 lines, the main Stop-hook regex classifier; note its version comments, e.g. v3/v4/v5 pattern extensions)
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/autonomy-posture-check-layer-4.2.sh (a SECOND Stop-hook layer, determine how it differs from / overlaps with autonomy-grant-stop-check.sh)
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/closure-discipline-check.sh
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/closure-discipline-signal-check.sh
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/closure-discipline-stop-check.sh
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/signal-recorder-silent-check.sh
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/presend-assertion-check.sh

Also read /Users/brien/.claude/settings.json to see exactly how these are wired (PreToolUse / Stop / SessionStart hook matchers).

For each hook determine: (1) is it PREVENTIVE (runs before/during generation, shapes what gets produced) or REACTIVE (runs on the Stop event, i.e. AFTER the full response text already exists, and can only append a correction or block submission)? (2) what does it pattern-match on: literal regex phrases, structural checks, or something else? (3) evidence of "whack-a-mole" evolution: version bumps, comments referencing prior misses, growing pattern lists. (4) redundancy/overlap between hooks. (5) is there ANY mechanism that intervenes BEFORE the model generates hedging/proposal-framing text, or does every control fire only after generation completes?

Report: a structured breakdown per hook (purpose, preventive vs reactive, trigger mechanism, size/complexity, notable version-drift evidence), then a summary verdict on whether this is architecturally capable of ever fully closing the gap it targets, given how it is built. Quote specific regex fragments or comments that reveal the whack-a-mole pattern if you find them.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'redesign_drafts',
    prompt: `Brien's Intent framework already contains an apparently-stalled NEXT-GENERATION redesign of the autonomy-grant enforcement system. Read these files in full:
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/autonomy-grant-enforcement.md (the CURRENT ratified spec the live hooks implement)
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/closure-discipline-enforcement.md (companion current spec)
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/autonomy-flight-model-v1-DRAFT.md
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/autonomy-flight-model-ratification-tracker.md
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/autonomy-gate-surface-matrix-v0-DRAFT.md
- ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/autonomy-posture-check-layer-4.2-DRAFT.md

Determine: (1) What does the current ratified spec actually prescribe, and does it match what the live hooks (analyzed by a sibling agent) implement, or has it drifted? (2) What does the DRAFT "flight model" / "gate surface matrix" redesign propose that is DIFFERENT from the current live system: is it a genuinely different architecture (e.g. preventive vs reactive), or just more of the same regex-classifier approach with more categories? (3) What is blocking these drafts from being ratified: read the ratification-tracker for explicit blockers, open items, or decision points awaiting Brien. (4) Your assessment: if the draft design were shipped as-is, would it plausibly close the pause-drift gap, or does it inherit the same structural weakness (reactive Stop-hook enforcement)?

Report your findings with direct citations (quote key passages) and an explicit verdict on whether the draft redesign is worth reviving/ratifying, needs modification, or should be abandoned in favor of a different architecture.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'signal_history',
    prompt: `Analyze the real incident history of "autonomy-grant pause-drift" (Claude unnecessarily asking permission / offering to do reversible local work instead of just doing it, despite an explicit standing grant) in Brien's Workspaces repo. Read these 14 signal files in full (this is the deduplicated canonical set; hundreds of other grep hits on this topic are stale duplicate copies living inside old git worktrees under .claude/worktrees/ and should be ignored):
- ${WS}/.context/signals/2026-04-19-cross-session-ledger-observability.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-03-adhd-session-context-hook.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-07-autonomy-grant-drift-sonnet-sessions.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-26-autonomy-grant-drift-recurrence-in-session.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-26-flight-model-ingestion.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-26-session-handoff-during-dentist.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-27-pause-at-scheduled-task-boundary.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-27-pause-drift-cross-reference-sweep-after-prompt-rework.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-27-pause-drift-items-3-6-7-8-after-coverage-push.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-27-pause-recurrence-immediately-after-meta-signal.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-27-paused-despite-autonomy-grant-drift-pattern.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-05-28-cast-library-day-completion.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-06-15-pause-drift-cvops-audit-standby-framing.md
- ${WS}/.intent/signals/SIG-2026-07-01-pause-drift-wave1-spike-softqueue.md

Also read these memory files if present: /Users/brien/.claude/projects/-Users-brien-Workspaces/memory/feedback_autonomy_grant_drift_pattern.md, /Users/brien/.claude/projects/-Users-brien-Workspaces/memory/feedback_pause_drift_meta_signal_on_nudge.md, /Users/brien/.claude/projects/-Users-brien-Workspaces/memory/feedback_post_stage_handoff.md.

Build a timeline: date, what triggered the pause, which specific hedging phrase/pattern was used ("say the word", "would you like me to", implicit queue, standby framing, etc.), whether the hook caught it (true positive, post-hoc correction) or missed it entirely (Brien caught it), and what "fix" was applied afterward (regex extension, new memory entry, new signal).

Then answer: is the RATE of new incidents declining over time (April had 3, May had 12, June+July combined had ~2 by this count), and if so, does that mean the problem is actually being solved, or does it mean signal-capture itself has lapsed while the underlying behavior continues unlogged (i.e. Brien is hitting this live in conversation right now, per his current complaint, without a fresh signal having been filed for recent occurrences)? Give your honest read on which explanation fits better given the timeline, and flag it clearly if you think under-logging rather than genuine improvement is the likelier explanation.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'parallax_governance',
    prompt: `Read the Parallax product's governance/decision-quality material in full:
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/spec/2026-05-31-governance-layer-vsm.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/spec/2026-06-05-gauntlet-disconfirmation-engine.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/spec/2026-06-05-decision-reconsideration-capability.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/spec/2026-06-01-scout-capability-design.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/spec/2026-06-02-overwatch-charter.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/spec/2026-04-28-three-axis-pipeline-architecture.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/spec/2026-06-02-portfolio-taxonomy.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/ARCHITECTURE.md
- ${WS}/Core/products/parallax/INTENT.md

Parallax is Brien's product/framework for how decisions and software get built with AI assistance (Viable-System-Model-style governance layer, S1-S5 autonomy gradient, disconfirmation/gauntlet testing of decisions, decision-reconsideration).

Determine: does ANY of this Parallax governance material reference, integrate with, or reason about the Intent framework's autonomy-grant hook mechanism (the 4-gate reversibility check, the Stop-hook enforcement) at ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/? Or are these two systems (Parallax = decision-quality governance for WHAT to build; Intent hooks = behavioral enforcement for HOW Claude should act moment-to-moment) currently siloed from each other with no cross-reference?

Specifically check the governance-layer-vsm.md S3/S4/S5 autonomy-gradient language against the Intent framework L0-L4 autonomy grant levels: are these the same concept modeled twice, or genuinely distinct? Report explicit findings on integration gaps, and whether Parallax's disconfirmation/gauntlet apparatus (used to pressure-test decisions) has ever been pointed at the autonomy-grant hook design itself.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'entire_and_skills',
    prompt: `Investigate two things.

1. ENTIRE CLI integration. Entire (entire.io) is a git-session-checkpoint tool installed on this machine (confirmed via 'entire --version' returning Entire CLI 0.5.1; it maintains .entire/ dirs and shadow branches/checkpoints per-repo, with commands: configure, enable, doctor, explain, resume, rewind, reset, status). Run READ-ONLY commands only: 'entire status' and 'entire doctor' from ${WS} (do not run configure/enable/reset/disable, those mutate state). Also grep the Intent hooks directory (${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/) and specs directory (${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/) for any mention of "entire" (case-insensitive) to determine whether the autonomy-grant 4-gate reasoning (Gate 1: "reversible?") currently accounts for the fact that Entire gives near-free checkpoint/rewind reversibility for local file work. If Entire provides trivial undo for almost all local Workspaces edits, does the CURRENT hook/spec design leverage that fact to make Gate-1 (reversibility) pass more confidently/automatically, or is this connection entirely unmade?

2. SKILLS layer. Read /Users/brien/.claude/skills/autonomy-grant-drift-detector/SKILL.md in full, this is a SessionStart-loaded "behavioral discipline" skill meant to catch drift toward proposal-framing. Also locate and read the build-intake-mandatory skill (search /Users/brien/.claude/skills/ for it) and the relevant autonomy/closure sections of the overwatch skill (search /Users/brien/.claude/skills/overwatch/ or similar, or Core/products/forge for a rendered copy).

Determine: these skills operate purely via PROMPT TEXT loaded into context (relying on the model reading and self-applying instructions), contrast this with the hook layer (mechanical regex enforcement on the Stop event). Is the skill layer redundant with the hook layer, or does it cover something hooks structurally cannot (shaping generation BEFORE the fact, vs hooks which only catch AFTER)? Report concretely on whether a prompt-based preventive layer exists at all, or whether prevention has never actually been attempted and every control is post-hoc.`,
  },
]

const mapResults = await parallel(mapTasks.map(t => () =>
  agent(t.prompt, { label: `map:${t.key}`, phase: 'Map' }).then(r => ({ key: t.key, result: r }))
))
const findings = {}
mapResults.filter(Boolean).forEach(r => { findings[r.key] = r.result })
log(`Map phase complete: ${Object.keys(findings).length}/5 tracks returned`)

phase('Diagnose')
const DIAGNOSIS_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  properties: {
    root_causes: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'object', properties: { cause: { type: 'string' }, evidence: { type: 'string' }, confidence: { type: 'string' } }, required: ['cause', 'evidence', 'confidence'] } },
    is_architecturally_reactive_only: { type: 'boolean' },
    stalled_redesign_assessment: { type: 'string' },
    signal_trend_interpretation: { type: 'string' },
    parallax_integration_gap: { type: 'string' },
    entire_leverage_gap: { type: 'string' },
    summary: { type: 'string' },
  },
  required: ['root_causes', 'is_architecturally_reactive_only', 'stalled_redesign_assessment', 'signal_trend_interpretation', 'parallax_integration_gap', 'entire_leverage_gap', 'summary'],
}

const diagnosisPrompt = `You are synthesizing five independent research tracks into a root-cause diagnosis of why Brien (the user) keeps experiencing "the AI forgets my autonomy grants and pauses for unnecessary permission" despite a large, actively-maintained enforcement system (Stop hooks, specs, skills, signals).

TRACK 1 - Hook mechanism forensics:
${findings.hooks}

TRACK 2 - Stalled redesign draft specs:
${findings.redesign_drafts}

TRACK 3 - Signal/incident history timeline:
${findings.signal_history}

TRACK 4 - Parallax governance material cross-reference:
${findings.parallax_governance}

TRACK 5 - Entire.io tooling + skills-layer investigation:
${findings.entire_and_skills}

Synthesize a root-cause diagnosis. Be blunt and specific, cite which track each causal claim comes from. Do not soften findings to be diplomatic; Brien wants the real architectural verdict, not a balanced summary. Rank root causes by how much of the recurring behavior they explain. Remember the key technical constraint: Claude Code hooks only fire on discrete events (SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionEnd) - there is no hook that can intervene mid-generation of the free-text response itself. Factor that constraint into your causal analysis of why a Stop-hook-only architecture structurally cannot fully prevent the behavior, only catch it after the fact.`

const diagnosisRaw = await agent(diagnosisPrompt, { label: 'diagnose', phase: 'Diagnose', schema: DIAGNOSIS_SCHEMA })

phase('Verify Diagnosis')
const REFUTE_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  properties: {
    refuted: { type: 'boolean' },
    corrections: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    notes: { type: 'string' },
  },
  required: ['refuted', 'corrections', 'notes'],
}
const diagnosisVotes = await parallel([
  () => agent(`Adversarially review this root-cause diagnosis of a chronic AI-behavior problem. Try to REFUTE it or find factual errors, unsupported leaps, or missing counter-evidence. The diagnosis:\n\n${JSON.stringify(diagnosisRaw, null, 2)}\n\nBe skeptical by default: if the underlying research tracks do not actually support a claim, flag it. If you find nothing wrong, return refuted:false and explain why it holds.`, { label: 'refute:diagnosis:1', phase: 'Verify Diagnosis', schema: REFUTE_SCHEMA }),
  () => agent(`Second, independent adversarial pass on the same diagnosis. Look specifically for whether it is too generous to the existing hook system (understates how broken it is) OR too harsh (ignores real mitigating evidence, like any signal-rate decline). The diagnosis:\n\n${JSON.stringify(diagnosisRaw, null, 2)}\n\nReturn refuted:true with corrections if you find a real problem, else refuted:false.`, { label: 'refute:diagnosis:2', phase: 'Verify Diagnosis', schema: REFUTE_SCHEMA }),
])

const diagnosisCorrections = diagnosisVotes.filter(Boolean).filter(v => v.refuted).flatMap(v => v.corrections)
log(`Diagnosis verification: ${diagnosisCorrections.length} corrections raised`)

phase('Design')
const DESIGN_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  properties: {
    name: { type: 'string' },
    description: { type: 'string' },
    mechanism: { type: 'string' },
    addresses_root_causes: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    implementation_steps: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    risks: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    feasibility_given_hook_constraints: { type: 'string' },
    feasibility_score: { type: 'number' },
  },
  required: ['name', 'description', 'mechanism', 'addresses_root_causes', 'implementation_steps', 'risks', 'feasibility_given_hook_constraints', 'feasibility_score'],
}

const diagnosisContext = `DIAGNOSIS:\n${JSON.stringify(diagnosisRaw, null, 2)}\n\nVERIFICATION CORRECTIONS TO FACTOR IN:\n${diagnosisCorrections.length ? diagnosisCorrections.join('\n- ') : 'None, diagnosis held up under adversarial review.'}`

const DESIGN_CANDIDATES = [
  {
    key: 'prevention_first',
    angle: `Design a "prevention-first" redesign. Given Claude Code hooks CANNOT intercept mid-generation text (only discrete events: SessionStart/PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop/SessionEnd), the only real preventive lever is shaping what goes INTO context before generation starts, or restructuring the interaction so the ambiguous free-text moment never has to happen (e.g. forcing a structured pre-response gate-check the model must emit and act on, not just read). Propose a concrete mechanism that acts on the INPUT side of generation rather than policing the OUTPUT after the fact. Be specific about what changes at SessionStart, in the system prompt injection, or in how the 4-gate check is elicited, versus the current all-reactive Stop-hook approach.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'consolidation',
    angle: `Design a "consolidation and retirement" redesign. The current system has at least two overlapping Stop-hook layers (autonomy-grant-stop-check.sh at 664 lines and autonomy-posture-check-layer-4.2.sh at 359 lines) plus three separate closure-discipline hooks, all growing via regex whack-a-mole (v3, v4, v5 pattern extensions). There is also an already-drafted-but-unratified "flight model" / "gate surface matrix" redesign sitting idle. Propose a concrete plan to collapse redundant layers, decide what to do with the stalled draft specs (revive, rewrite, or retire), and stop the regex-pattern-list from growing unboundedly. Be concrete about which files get merged, deleted, or ratified, and what replaces the whack-a-mole pattern-matching approach.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'tooling_leverage',
    angle: `Design a "tooling leverage" redesign centered on the Entire CLI (entire.io) checkpoint/shadow-branch/rewind capability already installed in this environment. If Entire gives near-free reversibility for local file work, propose how that fact should be wired directly into the autonomy-grant 4-gate reasoning (specifically Gate 1: "reversible?") so that for the large class of local reversible Workspaces work, the gate auto-resolves with high confidence instead of requiring the model to reason it out fresh (and hedge) every time. Be concrete: what would change in the SessionStart hook, the spec language, or the skill text to make "Entire has a checkpoint, so this is trivially undoable" a first-class, load-bearing fact rather than an unstated assumption.`,
  },
]

const designResults = await pipeline(
  DESIGN_CANDIDATES,
  d => agent(`${d.angle}\n\nContext you must ground this design in:\n${diagnosisContext}\n\nReturn a complete, concrete design, not a vague direction. Implementation steps should name actual files/mechanisms where possible given this is Claude Code (hooks live in ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/, specs in ${WS}/Core/frameworks/intent/spec/, skills in /Users/brien/.claude/skills/).`, { label: `design:${d.key}`, phase: 'Design', schema: DESIGN_SCHEMA }),
  (design, d) => agent(`Adversarially stress-test this proposed redesign for feasibility and effectiveness given Claude Code's real hook constraints (only SessionStart/PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop/SessionEnd events exist; no mid-generation interception is possible). Try to refute that it would actually reduce pause-drift, or find a way it could be gamed, ignored, or fail the same way the current system fails. Design:\n\n${JSON.stringify(design, null, 2)}\n\nReturn refuted:true with corrections if you find a real problem, else refuted:false.`, { label: `verify:${d.key}`, phase: 'Verify Design', schema: REFUTE_SCHEMA }).then(v => ({ key: d.key, design, verify: v }))
)

phase('Synthesize')
const synthesisPrompt = `Write a complete audit-and-design report for Brien on why his Intent-framework autonomy-grant enforcement system chronically fails to prevent unnecessary pause/proposal-framing on work he has already granted standing autonomy for, and what to do about it.

Inputs:

DIAGNOSIS (post-adversarial-review):
${diagnosisContext}

CANDIDATE REDESIGNS WITH ADVERSARIAL VERIFICATION:
${JSON.stringify(designResults, null, 2)}

Write the report in markdown with these sections:
1. Executive Summary (3-5 sentences, lead with the verdict, not the backstory)
2. Root Cause Diagnosis (ranked causes with evidence, be blunt)
3. Why The Current Architecture Cannot Fully Solve This (the reactive-only hook constraint, explained plainly)
4. The Stalled Redesign (what the existing DRAFT flight-model/gate-matrix specs already propose, and why they never shipped)
5. Three Candidate Fixes, Evaluated (summarize each design, its adversarial verification result, and a feasibility verdict)
6. Recommendation (pick ONE primary path, or an explicit combination, with a one-line why; this is Brien's decision-making style: recommendation-first, never a bare choice)
7. Concrete Action Plan (numbered, phased, naming actual files to create/edit/retire/ratify)
8. What This Means For Parallax (the governance-layer-vsm S3-S5 autonomy gradient vs Intent L0-L4 grant levels integration gap, if the research found one)
9. Proposed DDR Stub (a short draft: Context / Decision / Alternatives Considered / Consequences / Validation Criteria, ready for Brien to ratify or amend, per the Workspaces DDR convention)

Style rules (these are Brien's hard requirements, follow them exactly): no em-dashes, no en-dashes, no arrow glyphs, no ellipsis character anywhere in the document. Be direct and specific, name real tradeoffs, no hedging, no filler. Do not claim anything is "resolved" or "fixed", this is an audit and design proposal only, nothing has been implemented yet. Return ONLY the finished markdown report body, no preamble.`

const report = await agent(synthesisPrompt, { label: 'synthesize', phase: 'Synthesize' })

return { diagnosis: diagnosisRaw, diagnosisVerification: diagnosisVotes, designs: designResults, report }

<summary>Dynamic workflow "Full audit of Intent/Parallax autonomy-grant + closure-discipline hook fabric to diagnose chronic pause-drift and design a fix" completed</summary>
<result>{"diagnosis":{"root_causes":[{"cause":"Structural blind spot: the ONLY enforcement layer that inspects free-text conversational hedging (proposal-framing, bare-choice, soft-queue, silent-stop) is the Stop hook, which by Claude Code's event model fires strictly AFTER the full response is already generated and appended to the transcript. There is no PreToolUse-equivalent for the model's own prose; PreToolUse only sees structured tool_input payloads (subagent dispatch prompts, file writes), never the conversational response text itself. So for the specific behavior Brien is complaining about (the AI pauses/asks permission in its own words), the entire fabric is architecturally reactive-only: catch, block re-submission, force a rewrite. It cannot prevent the first token of 'Want me to...' from being written, only notice the sentence after it's complete and demand a redo.","evidence":"TRACK 1: explicit walk of all 9 hooks concludes 'of the four places drift is actually caught... all of them are Stop-hook, i.e., after-the-fact...
